data.title: Synopsys: Fundamentally Strong With Limited Upside
data.description:
Today, Synopsys (NASDAQ:SNPS) is the unequivocal leader in the electronic design automation (EDA) market, developing software solutions for the automation of chip design. An important part of the company's business is licensing IP blocks, such as USB interfaces and memory controllers, which become building blocks for customers like Nvidia, Intel, and Qualcomm. In recent years, Synopsys management has shifted its focus from the core business to new development vectors, including AI and multichip systems. The final result of the work on AI was the recently introduced AgentEngineer tool for automating chip design using AI technologies. An even more important development was the $35 billion purchase of Ansys to combine EDA capabilities with engineering simulation. In this article, I will look at how the Synopsys business model works, what it earns, what the deal with Ansys means for shareholders, and how the company's financial position changes after this transaction. Based on that, I'll provide the fair value of the business in the current environment.

Business Overview

The company generates revenue by selling software for automating the design of microcircuits (called EDA for short), and also earns licenses from intellectual property – that is, from ready-made microcircuit blocks (Design IP). In fact, without Synopsys and its software, the development of modern chips can be said to be impossible.

Now, management is changing the operational business model, gradually moving from one-time software sales to a subscription or rental approach for Synopsys software. This approach is a win-win situation for both parties, as it provides additional flexibility and access to the latest versions of solutions for clients while providing more predictable cash flows for Synopsys.

The Design Automation segment is the backbone of the business and accounts for 65-70% of all revenue. These are all tools for logic synthesis, simulation, and verification of chips. This segment has an operating margin of up to 40%. Against this background, the IP direction is a younger, but also a faster-growing asset with a growth rate of 23-25% due to high demand for next-generation interfaces: PCIe 6.0, DDR5, accelerators, and network cores. This share of the total income structure is about 30%, and it continues to gain weight. The model works differently here: the company sells IP licenses and receives royalties from each chip produced by the customer — that is, the share of revenue increases with the scale of implementation.

In general, the company is now striving to develop cloud versions of its software, introduce AI into the workflow, and collaborate with industry giants, such as Taiwan's TSMC on 3DIC solutions and UCIe multichip packaging.

Ansys Acquisition In The Works

Synopsys management announced its desire to buy Ansys in early 2024 and is still negotiating with regulators to complete it. Most key regulators have already approved the merger (subject to certain conditions, such as the sale by Keysight Technologies), so a deal breakdown is unlikely.

The deal is valued at approximately $35 billion, which is equivalent to $197 in cash plus 0.345 Synopsys shares for each Ansys share. Such terms mean Synopsys would pay about 30% of the premium to Ansys shareholders for the purchase of the company and, upon closing of the transaction, Ansys owners will receive approximately 16.5% of the new combined business. The remaining 83.5% will remain with the shareholders of Synopsys currently. In total, Synopsys must pay Ansys approximately $19 billion in cash and issue its shares; this $19 billion will be covered by a combination of equity and debt financing.

Evaluating this transaction, I see both the pros and cons of its implementation from the shareholders' point of view. Synopsys management is looking at the deal very positively, expecting the combined company to maintain double-digit revenue growth, and the combined margin and free cash flow to grow through synergies. Synopsys estimates the synergies at about $400 million per year (in the form of cost reductions and new sales) with the prospect of growing to over $1 billion in the future.

This sounds very good, but the implementation of such plans will not be easy. In M&A transactions, sellers usually benefit, and the buying party finds itself in a situation where the debt burden is often quite high while the synergy effects are not what was expected. Before the merger, Synopsys had virtually no debt (at the end of FY2024, cash was approximately $4.05 billion with a debt of only $15.6 million). But by Q2 FY2025, about $10 billion of new debt was involved. As a result, the net debt to EBITDA ratio may increase to 5-6x after the fact, which carries the risk of increased financial expenses. Management promises to aggressively reduce debt: according to forecasts, within 2 years after closing, the debt–to-adjusted EBITDA ratio will decrease to 2x, and the long-term goal is about 1x while maintaining the investment grade. Thus, a deal for shareholders can be both very good and problematic. I'm still leaning towards a positive outcome.

Strong 2Q 2025 Performance

The second quarter of this year turned out to be quite good for the company. Revenue increased by 10.3% compared to last year's level. Yet, there is still pressure from export restrictions in China. However, due to high demand in Europe and Korea, Synopsys managed to increase revenue. The main growth occurred in the Design IP segment, plus 20-21% year-on-year, especially as a result of new generation interfaces such as SerDes, PCIe, and Ethernet. The Design Automation segment grew more modestly, about 6-7%, but remained dominant in terms of revenue.

Operating profit also increased, with an operating margin of about 23.5% or 38% non-GAAP, which remains at the level of the previous quarter. Economies of scale work here: R&D and infrastructure costs grow slower than revenue, which supports profitability. GAAP net income reached $349.2 million, or $2.24 per share, which is 17% higher than last year's results. However, the main picture is reflected by non-GAAP data, cleared of one-time expenses and depreciation; here, profit was $572.7 million, and EPS was $3.67 versus $3.00 a year earlier. The results are explained not only by revenue growth but also by maintaining high operational efficiency. The non-GAAP net profit margin exceeded 35%, while GAAP remained at 21.8% — the gap is due to depreciation and tax adjustments.

Thus, Synopsys remains confident in its financial trajectory. Management confirmed its annual revenue forecast of $6.745–6.805 billion, citing continued high demand driven by the growth of AI-oriented architectures and multichip solutions. The business remains consistently profitable, and the company continues to demonstrate high operating margins. Management has confirmed an operating margin target above 35% under non-GAAP for the entire 2025 fiscal year. Despite additional pressure related to the costs of the Ansys deal and restructuring programs, the company remains optimistic about its net profit. The EPS forecast for the full year is $15.11–15.19, and for the third quarter — $3.82–3.87.

Robust FCF and Valuation with Limited Upside

To calculate the fair value of the company's shares, I use a DCF model with a forecast of UFCF until 2030. When calculating the terminal cost, I use three approaches: the P/E multiplier method, the EV/EBITDA method, and the perpetuity growth method. This allows the estimation of a range of possible fair value values depending on the selected parameters and the level of the discount rate (WACC).

I expect that in the next five years, the company will be able to keep FCF in the range of 18-20% of revenue and reach $2.3 billion by 2030. This forecast is based primarily on a gradual slowdown in revenue growth as the market becomes saturated. This scenario corresponds to an average annual growth of about 11% in the period 2025-2030. By comparison, Synopsys' revenue has grown by an average of 13% per year over the past five years, but part of this increase is due to acquisitions and the rapid growth phase of EDA/automation. I assume that in the future, Synopsys will follow the growth rate of the semiconductor industry, but will not be able to consistently maintain a 20-30% growth rate. In general, my forecasts are close to the consensus forecasts.

As for operating profit, I expect margins of about 23-24% GAAP and 38-40% non-GAAP in the next five years. This is close to the company's current level and long-term expectations (for example, the expected margin expansion of 1.25 percentage points after the merger with Ansys).

In the past, Synopsys held CapEx for about 2-3% of revenue. This is a fairly low level (which is typical for software companies). In my forecast, I take CapEx at about 3.0% of revenue annually, assuming that Synopsys will invest in maintaining its software platforms and possible R&D infrastructure, but will not require large investments (for example, in physical assets) above this share. These elements are built into the model and explain the forecast of stable free cash flow growth in the long term.

Let's move on to WACC. To calculate the Cost of equity, I took the beta for the last 12 months at 1.16x, the Equity risk premium at 4.3%, according to Professor Damodaran, and for the risk-free rate I took the yield of 10-Year US government bonds yield. I use the latest TTM Interest Expense divided by the latest one-year quarterly average debt to get the simplified cost of debt. Thus, according to my calculations, the company's WACC was about 8.5%.

Let's consider the results of all three methods of calculating the share price:

  • P/E multiplier with a range of 29–33x and a WACC discount of 7.5–9.5%. Under the baseline scenario (WACC 8.5%, P/E 31x), the fair share price estimate is about $493, which gives an upside of about 8% of the current price ($455).
  • EV/EBITDA multiplier (25-29x), where under the same assumptions we get a target price in the region of $421, that is, a downside of 8%.
  • Perpetuity growth method with a growth rate of 2.0–3.0% and the same WACC range. With the basic parameters (growth rate 2.5%, WACC 8.5%), the fair price estimate is exactly $459 per share — an upside of only 1%.

Additionally, the charts above visualize fair prices and growth potential at various multipliers and discount rates. It can be seen that with an EV/EBITDA of 25x and a WACC of 9.5%, the shares are valued at $376 or a -17% downside. This is essentially the lowest estimate among all three valuation methods. Similarly, the P/E model at 29x and a high WACC of 9.5% shows a drop to $441, with a downside of about 3%. Thus, the downside at conservative input levels is 17% of the current levels. Conversely, if we take the most positive figures, such as EV/EBITDA 29x and WACC 7.5% or P/E 33x and WACC 7.5%, then estimates will be at $469 (+3%) and $550 (+21%).

I took the basic scenario for each valuation method and calculated the average price among all three methods and got the fair value of the company's shares at $457, which is very close to the current price and, accordingly, without any upside. In general, I agree with this assessment, the multipliers in my model are quite conservative, and the current multipliers are very high (P/E TTM – 52.6x and EV/EBITDA – 45.57x). I am not ready to buy companies with such multipliers. But at the same time, I don't see any obvious factors or threats to the fall in the share price at the moment, except if the deal with Ansys suddenly breaks down. And so, it would be logical to assign a Hold rating to Synopsys shares. I was considering buying the stock when the price was corrected below $430.

Capital Return to Shareholders

Synopsys does not pay dividends, has not paid them, and is not going to pay them in the near future. Regarding share buybacks, Synopsys has periodically used such programs. As of May 2025, according to reports, there are about $194 million of unused buyback permits. However, there were no share buybacks in the last quarter: the number of shares repurchased was zero. Thus, the formed buyback program can be considered curtailed at the moment. No additional announcements have been made about new rounds of capital repatriation. In general, with major investment projects and upcoming commitments under the Ansys deal, Synopsys management is focused on maintaining its balance sheet and reducing debt, rather than returning capital through dividends or large-scale buybacks. Therefore, the parameters of the buyback/dividend programs remain modest – formally, the remainder of the share repurchase authorization remains, but there were almost no actual payments to shareholders in 2024-2025.

Risks

One of the most serious risks is the regulation of the sector. In May 2025, the company suddenly dropped its revenue and profit forecasts due to a letter from the US Department of Commerce explicitly forbidding the supply of software to China. And this, for a moment, is a key market for the entire semiconductor industry. At the same time, a deal with Ansys is still on the horizon. Regulators must approve it, and the FTC has already rolled out a condition: to sell part of the assets. The Chinese side has not yet given the green light either, which could greatly reduce the potential synergies from the deal. Against this background, the competition is also active — Cadence, Siemens, and a bunch of startups are on their heels, while Synopsys' business is quite narrow. The loss of one major customer can seriously affect revenue. Add to this the cyclical nature of the market itself — if demand for chips slows down, then so does demand for design tools. The debt burden is also stressful. After raising $10 billion in debt to purchase Ansys, the company turned out to be much more sensitive to how quickly and effectively, it can realize synergies. If there are delays or not everything goes according to plan, this will immediately affect profits and the ability to repay obligations. The last thing is a reassessment. Synopsys shares are trading at fairly high multiples, and in an unstable market, even a small negative news — whether it's macro, industry, or the business itself — can cause a sharp drop in the price.

Final Thoughts

Synopsys remains the undisputed technology leader in the field of EDA and IP, effectively scaling its business through the introduction of AI solutions and a strategic merger with Ansys. However, the high valuation of the company at the moment, the growing debt burden, and the continuing regulatory uncertainty, especially in the context of the transaction, limit the short-term upside. Financial performance remains strong, but implementing synergies takes time and a lot of work. Taking into account the neutral DCF assessment and macro and industry risks, I assign Synopsys shares a "Hold" recommendation, with potential buying interest at levels below $430.

data.title: IVW: Growth At An Unreasonable Price
data.description:
The second time I covered the iShares S&P 500 Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:IVW), I focused on the fact that the growth bias introduces higher risk because of the significant contribution of tech stocks to the fund's performance (around 40% of the fund is allocated to the sector). My problem was valuation and the AI euphoria that it reflected. As I said at the time:

Whenever a new invention is recognized by the market as transformative, the resulting enthusiasm often attracts far more capital than what the actual growth from this invention would justify.

A lot has changed since then. While the valuation is still unattractive and the market still doesn't care about potential issues surrounding AI adoption, rapid trade policy shifts on a global scale have so far resulted in poor market returns this year.

In this article, I want to expand on the developments a bit and capture the rest of the issues that make IVW a dangerous fund to hold right now.

But let's review the basics first.

Portfolio

As I said, ~40% of the portfolio is in tech stocks, with communication and consumer cyclical ones taking another 27%. This is consistent with the fact that large companies in these sectors are growing at a faster pace than the rest of the index. The S&P 500 is currently one-third technology and about 14% Financials, making the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) appear conservative in comparison. Here are the sector weights of IVW:

One crucial detail you need to know is that NVIDIA (NVDA) has a 12.41% weight; that's almost double compared to SPY, introducing unusually high idiosyncratic risk. The rest of the holdings have more prudent allocations that are not so different than SPY's for the most part.

Performance

Although IVW had a more significant drawdown than SPY this year, it managed to outperform it recently after the markets recovered from the April low.

NVDA, which is the largest holding of IVW, can take a large part of the credit as it greatly outperformed the market in May.

The performance can be seen as especially impressive since even IVW has caught up to value stocks, which held up better as the tariff narrative took hold of the market.

Outlook

Monetary Policy

With CPI growth down to 2.31% and core PCE growth at 2.65% on a YoY basis, you may wonder, "What inflation problem?"

Indeed, progress has been made in the last few months on the inflation front; it appears that reaching the 2% target set by the Fed won't be so hard after all.

At the same time, GDP growth may have taken a hit from front-loading, but it's still at a solid level and nonfarm payroll growth, as well as the unemployment rate, doesn't indicate a labor market problem either.

Well, the last FOMC meeting and the corresponding Fed minutes indicate that the central bank isn't looking at the past anymore. The Fed's staff economists have revised their growth expectations lower and the labor market is now projected to “weaken substantially” within 2025, with the unemployment rate rising and remaining at a higher level for the next two years.

The idea is that the current trend of CPI/PCE readings and still solid growth data are not reliable sources of informing the decision of the committee, as they don't yet capture the rapid trade-policy shifts, which can be stagflationary. Therefore, the Fed prefers to wait for more data before it makes any change in the policy rate of interest.

Trade Policy

On May 29, the Court of International Trade ruled that President Trump lacked the authority to impose some of the tariffs that began the global trade war. This is good news for the markets, as the reciprocal and fentanyl-related tariffs were expected to materially change trade relationships and cause economic growth issues on a global scale.

Because the tariff narrative is the force behind the heightened volatility, this roadblock for Trump should provide enough fuel for IVW to continue its uptrend in the short run. My problem is that it's probably going to prove no more than a short-term boost, as the administration plans to appeal the ruling. In other words, I would be careful before I think this is over.

Technicals

Right now, the technical picture suggests that IVW's position is not favorable. Momentum has been weakened as suggested by an RSI drop from ~72 (the recent peak) to ~65, coinciding with Trump's 50% tariff announcement in regard to EU imports, as trade negotiations were progressing slowly. The nearly immediate delay of those threatened tariffs, as well as the court ruling mentioned above, helped the market bounce back.

However, the RSI is at a very high level, and any trade-policy development in Trump's favor could undo the recent recovery. The price is well above the 50DMA and is approaching its upper Bollinger band, set at a 2.5 standard deviation.

Valuation

Technicals are not the only indicators suggesting a high price level. Again, the valuation of IVW is a key issue for investors. Its P/E ratio sits at 33.63x and its P/B at 9.30. This is, of course, reflective of the high-tech concentration, but it can by no means be warranted under current conditions.

Such valuation levels make SPY seem undervalued; it has an earnings multiple of 22.55 and a book value premium of 4.68x. While I don't think that SPY should be bought at current levels either, the valuation differential helps to illustrate how aggressive IVW is and the potentially precarious state that investors who currently favor it are in.

Catalysts

On June 6, we will get nonfarm payroll data for May, which might be perceived as important by the market depending on the growth it will show. Moreover, on June 11, the May CPI reading will be published and will help us gauge if and what the impact of inflation fears was in May.

These dates might prove to be important catalysts for both market action and Fed policy later on. If the Fed is justified in being so careful regarding the impact of tariffs on growth and inflation so far, the next two weeks will be crucial for investors.

Verdict

All in all, the main risks are still there; valuation is high, and AI enthusiasm hasn't waned. The trade-policy risk is still strong, but I will have to admit that it looks to be losing its edge as the worst of the U.S.-China conflict seems to be behind us and Trump has some work to do before he gets back to using tariffs to achieve his goals.

Volatility should remain high, however, and though the risks and opportunities seem more balanced, that's an important problem for market participants. Being cautious continues to be a reasonable approach.

data.title: Radiant Logistics: The Stock Is Waiting For The Bullwhip Effect
data.description:
Radiant Logistics (NYSE:RLGT), a provider of logistics and multimodal transportation services, has seen its stock price decline 12.2% since my last report , reflecting the impact of ongoing trade disruptions. This drop underscores the vulnerability of freight and logistics companies in a deteriorating macroeconomic environment. In March 2025, I reiterated my buy rating on the stock based on expectations that trade negotiations would lead to a constructive long-term outcome or what the company’s CEO calls the “bullwhip effect”.

In this report, I review the Q3 2025 results, reassess my investment thesis, and provided an updated price target and rating based on the latest performance and macro backdrop.

Radiant Logistics Is Waiting For The Bullwhip

In a volatile trade environment, the share prices of freight and logistics companies come under pressure for the simple reason that trade wars can disturb demand and the flow of goods. In Q3 2025, revenues generated in Canada were $28.1 million accounting for 13.1% of all revenues. Perhaps somewhat counterintuitive, the sales were up from $27.8 million in Q2 when Canada represented around 10.5% of sales.

If we look at what trade actually has done so far to the business, the reality is that not a lot has happened. Year-on-year sales in the US also grew by roughly $29 million. That is not to say that there are no risks for Radiant Logistics but the impact so far has not been detectable in the company’s result, only in the share prices. So, fundamentally there has not been a major change in revenues but a significant downturn in investor sentiment resulted in lower share prices. This discrepancy may provide an opportunity for investors, which could be amplified if positive trade agreements are reached, not only with Canada but also with China.

Amid the trade turmoil, Radiant Logistics could also continue converting agent stations to fully owned stations which should benefit the company.

Radiant Logistics Profits Increase

Q3 2025 revenues improved from $184.6 million to $214 million. This 16% increase in sales only partially translated to the bottom line as variable costs in the form of cost for transportation services increased by $24.4 million and personnel costs increased by $1 million. Furthermore, depreciation and amortization increased and there were some lease termination costs. This left the company with a $3.3 million operating profit compared to a $0.9 million loss a year earlier. So, despite the cost increases and the trade turmoil profits improved, which I believe is largely driven by some capacity exiting the system leaving to better unit rates and the conversion of agent stations to fully owned stations leading to lower partner commissions.

Operating margins improved from -0.5% to 1.6%. While there is an improvement in the margins, it should be noted that the margins are thin as they are for many companies providing freight and logistics services. Some headwind can be absorbed through station conversion and taking variable costs out of the system, but besides that there is not a lot of margin to absorb shocks. So, Radiant Logistics has to carefully navigate the current macroeconomic backdrop. I believe that the Q3 2025 earnings show that they are doing that quite well realizing a year-on-year growth.

Radiant Logistics Stock Is Undervalued Against Its TTM and Forward Earnings.

To determine multi-year price targets The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Radiant Logistics, I am revising the stock price target from $8.46 to $8.63 driven by a 5% increase in the EBITDA estimate for this year, positioning the company a little bit better for next year and a lower annualized M&A path this year and next year. So, I am maintaining my buy rating for the stock.

Conclusion: Radiant Logistics Navigates A Complex Macroeconomic Environment

The financial results somewhat, perhaps somewhat surprisingly, improved year-on-year. The trade environment has prevailed in investor sentiment, but what we currently see reflected in the earnings is an improved unit revenue environment that many freight and logistics companies have been waiting for.

In case, trade tensions ease I do believe there is significant upside ahead. So, the company’s stock remains a higher risk buy.

data.title: Repsol: Further Upside In Spanish Oil/Energy Possible
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

For some months now, I have been adding to European-based energy stocks, especially once their share prices grew weaker due to the pressure from the global energy market. Repsol is hardly my first or largest position to increase or to focus on, but it's part of my European energy portfolio. I have covered the company twice over the past 8 months, with my latest article being found here.

Today, I mean to use the Q1 2025 results to update my thesis for the company and show you why Repsol remains attractive to me, for the same reason that there are energy companies in the sector that remain attractive to me, provided their valuation is good.

Repsol has "what you'd want" out of an energy stock. With operations spanning the Iberian Peninsula and internationally, including the South American continent, the company has operations ranging from the upstream segment to industrial production, to low-carbon generation. While not fully vertically integrated, and with some uncertainty or volatility added to by the addition of petrochemical operations, the company is nonetheless worth looking at and investing in at the right price. Almost 100 years of operational history make this company laced with history and tradition as well, and I believe it's going nowhere.

Let's see what the latest results brought us.

Repsol - Upside from combined upstream/energy and petrochemicals in Q1 2025 to 2025-2027

The results I am referring to are now about a month old – plenty of time for the market to digest them, and if you see what the market has done in about a month, you will note that the market didn't like what it saw – at least initially, the company has recovered fairly quickly, all things considered.

Why did it like what it saw?

It has to do with what I've covered that the company is doing. Repsol is pushing more into low-carbon generation investments – investments that are currently very costly, and won't show a clear bottom line return for the foreseeable future. This is weighing on results and on the "mood" about Repsol. It should be clearly noted that the investments mentioned are required for the company to hit its emissions targets. However, this "math" will also mean that for the next few years, only oil and gas businesses will be the contributing sectors for cash and earnings for the next few years. And as we've seen in the last few months, these can be, and are, volatile. Therefore, the somewhat negative mood about the earnings, at least initially, was understandable because it was little more than confirmation of the strategy.

Upstream is unlikely to grow going forward – this was confirmed by Q1 2025 as well. The cash generated here will be used to fund investments and shareholder returns. This means that production of crude will remain flat, but likely also decline somewhat due to the nature of how oil/drilling works (the longer the well is active, the harder it is to get the same amount of oil with the same amount of work). What will likely add to the bottom line instead of pure volume is new higher-margin segments, which will have to contribute the heavy lifting here.

The company generated €0.7B of adjusted quarterly income and over €1.5B of pre-WC operational cash flow. Repsol remains in an extremely attractive fundamental position, with less than 17% gearing, making it one of the least geared companies in this entire sector. If we exclude the effect of IFRS 17 leases, the company's gearing is below 6%. Repsol has done many things to "get here".

For Q1, the company's bottom line was affected by seasonal build-up of WC, which usually comes in Q1, and a net capex that is reflective of the company's strategy. The company is also continuing to divest, with €0.7B targeted and €0.4B already cashed out as of April 2025.

The company's progress in its plans has also been impressive. A new JV with Neo Energy in the UK, asset rotation in Spain and the U.S., and a full-year dividend for 2025 that came in 8.3% higher, with a solid dividend for 2026, confirms the company's trajectory.

Confirming undervaluation, the company has also announced buyback plans, about €350M before the tail end of July 2025, which will and has been supporting the valuation and share price.

It's indisputable to me that the company is generating value through asset rotation. Even with this in the base scenario, and even assuming the continued rather "unstable" macro for energy, Repsol is not in any fundamental danger whatsoever. The shareholder remuneration increases reflect an increasingly certain outlook for the company, less dependent on macro, and even with a net CapEx reduced down to €3.5B due to macro factors, operational cash flow is unlikely to come in at less than €6B – confirmed by the company in 2025, even in a stressed scenario, generating production of 530–550 Kboped.

Similarly, the company's industrial segment can increase its CFFO through efficiency, growing the trading segment, and improving the portfolio quality, with higher-margin renewable fuels, in both its home market and potentially in the U.S. as well. As it stands, the company's lower-carbon businesses seem very likely to become a major value driver in 2027, at which point those including retail will represent nearly 35% of employed capital, with over 40% 3 years after. Those countercyclical operations will at this point contribute over 15% of the company's CFFO on an annual basis if things go as planned. Turning a company like Repsol into this is a relatively long-term project, but as it stands, and confirmed by 2025 Q1, the process is ongoing.

The bullish arguments, updated for the quarter, include the company's very tight focus on upstream – meaning it's incredibly selective in what projects it goes into. The company's investment in renewable operations, not just solar farms and the like, but also things like biofuels, continues to add value over time, and will likely be supportive of higher margins. This is especially true in the EU, where these operations are considered crucial. It's clear to me, as it was when I looked over Repsol to begin with, that the company's shareholder return plan holds water even in a crude scenario of $50–$55/bbl – and this continues to form the basis and backing for my positive thesis for the company.

Risks to the company, on the other hand, will continue to be the drawdown/reduction in its smaller and smaller upstream position, meaning the benefit from oil/gas prices is likely to be smaller than expected. It's further likely that the recent strong results, including Q1 2025 because they are dependent more on record refining margins, are likely to go down, not up, if the global economy continues to weaken or go down. Furthermore, as you may recall, I'm not that "Gung-ho" about renewable investments for the sake of renewable investments. The sector is unattractive (in my view), and the company's limited experience compared to some of the Goliaths in the sector might result in lower earnings and result as well. What I am saying is that Repsol is being discounted, by me, for renewable results being far lower than expected.

All of these factors explain why Repsol may be a buy at this price, but why I view peers as sometimes more attractive if their valuation upside is better.

Let me show you the valuation case as of May/June 2025.

Valuation for Repsol - Maintaining PT, but being more careful

It's important to state that this company is not my main play in energy, or energy "Europe". It's equally important to state, however, that I believe Repsol is being vastly underrated by a market that's grown negative on energy (at least somewhat). The company is further added to by the 2021–2022 impact of record pricing and asset sales, during which EPS skyrocketed to over €4.6, compared to a usual level of about a quarter or a third of that EPS.

European oil majors and integrated oil and gas companies overall trade at between 6–7x P/E. This is low, I agree, but it's generally what you'll find the 4–5 year averages currently stand. This completely disregards the company's tendency to outperform often, as you can see below.

More importantly, however, what it disregards is the BBB+ credit, the yield which is now close to 7%, and the fact that even at 6.17x normalized, the 5-year average, you're raking in a 20%+ annualized RoR here.

So even in a base or bearish case, the company has significant return potential. My continued price target – that's price target, not fair value – of €17/share reflects my conviction that European oil majors long-term are likely to trade closer to 8–9x P/E once their plans materialize. This includes Total (TTE), Eni (E), Equinor (EQNR), and others, many of which I invest in and hold significant longer-term positions in. Repsol is but one of many here for me, and this is based on a long-term conviction of upside. At a 9–10x forward P/E, which by the way is the 20-year average for Repsol, Repsol's implied fair value climbs to €21-25/share, and the annualized RoR comes to over 40%. Clearly, this is a bullish thesis, but let's not forget what the company has done in the past, and there is support for such a thesis.

Repsol is, in my view, a very good long-term investment. You should not buy it for the short term, given the likely EPS shortfall this year, currently 20% negative or above is being forecasted, but this is likely to turn to growth in 2026.

When it comes to peers, one could argue that others are equally, or more, attractive. I will not consider U.S./NA-based peers to be relevant here – they are in a different environment. Main peers are, as I noted above, those mentioned in addition to Shell (SHEL) and BP (BP). I view Total, BP, Shell, Repsol, and Equinor all to be undervalued to various degrees. Out of all, Repsol has the best yield, but also the most uncertain mix. BP, Shell, and Total have the best "plans" in my view, which is why, together with Eni, they represent the majority of my investments here. Repsol is attractive, but as to which company is most attractive, that will depend on what asset mix and plan you want. Repsol is further attractive to me because I am more fully invested in the mentioned peers, but not yet in Repsol.

So based on this, I will forecast Repsol at a 2027–2028 8–9x P/E, which comes to a significant upside using a €21 fair value, and a PT of €17/share or below. That comes to a significant upside from today's price of below €12/share, and the company is therefore a "BUY". For the ADR REPYY this comes to $19.2/share.

Thesis

  • Repsol is one of the leading players in Europe and Ibero-America. It has attractive, fundamentally safe operations with good diversification across 4 attractive segments, even with the potential plan to list its upstream segment. Its renewable ambitions will result in the company becoming one of the leading, diversified players in this field, and this is a non-trivial upside in a better environment than this. Q1 2025 confirms my positive bullish stance, and I continue to forecast it at a much higher return assumption than other investors seem to be doing here.
  • The company adds to this with a shareholder-friendly dividend and buyback policy, as evidenced by the stated shareholder yield. As an investor, you will not be left out in the cold, partaking in the company's profit and cash flows.
  • Based on the attractive valuation, making the risk/reward ratio an attractive one, I believe Repsol warrants a "Buy" at this price and in this situation. I put my price target at least €17/share, and this is the absolutely lowest I'm willing to go, with a "Buy" rating. This continues to be my rating in 2025 after Q1 2025.

My short-term investment case (6-12 months) is as follows:

The short-term thesis is likely to be impacted by divestments, resulting in short-term lower returns, but I believe the right way to look at the company is to look at the longer-term potential here. Short-term investors should be somewhat more careful here, and I would, in May/June, still not go in for the short term.

My medium- to long-term investment case (up to and beyond 5 years) is as follows:

I believe Repsol is fundamentally undervalued in a way that justifies an upside to at the very least a 6x–8x P/E, below the other European oil majors, and this calls for an upside of around 30–40% from today's share price.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €17/share where I see a risk/reward adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of May/June 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Instacart: Navigating An Increasingly Crowded Market
data.description:
Maplebear Inc. (NASDAQ:CART), better known as Instacart, operates an online grocery delivery platform in the U.S. and Canada. The Instacart platform connects local grocery retailers with consumers by offering home delivery services. Instacart has grown into one of the largest online grocery companies by market share with an approximated 21.6% market share in 2024.

After an IPO in 2023, Instacart’s stock has now returned quite well. While there are rising concerns regarding Instacart’s ability to capture market growth due to increased competition, the company continues to generate very healthy cash flow alongside fair topline expansion.

Instacart Grows and Earns, But Is Losing Market Share

Instacart’s financial performance looks good. The company has been able to grow revenues significantly in the past few years, enabled by great market-wide growth in online grocery deliveries. Instacart’s revenues currently stand at $3455 million over the past twelve months, growing at a rapid CAGR of 22.1% from 2020. The company has experienced great growth in active users, and ultimately in total orders. Significant food inflation through the 2022-2023 period has also aided revenue growth.

Earnings have also been good, as Instacart generates consistent transaction fees and advertising revenues from end users and companies. The company’s significantly largest operating cost item is sales and marketing to reach a growing number of consumers, while other expenses to operate the platform are quite moderate. While GAAP earnings have fluctuated much more, Instacart’s adjusted EBITDA margins are great, for example reaching a great 26.2% level in 2024. The company generates a great amount of free cash flow with a total of $750 million during the past twelve months.

While still growing well, Instacart’s recent growth is even concerningly low – the online grocery delivery market continues to show very rapid growth, still expected to grow at an 11% rate in 2026 with consistent growth afterwards. In comparison, Instacart’s Q1 gross transaction value “GTV” grew at a slightly slower 10% rate, and the company guides for just 8-10% GTV growth in Q2; Instacart’s growth is decelerating, and the company’s doesn’t seem able to fully capitalize on market growth.

Increased competitive pressure is Instacart’s primary issue. Notably, many grocery retailers have chosen to develop internal delivery services as the market opportunity has grown – Kroger (KR) offers its own delivery alongside Instacart delivery as an option, and Walmart (WMT) also has its own delivery service. Moreover, DoorDash (DASH) is applying competitive pressure by trying to pursue growth in grocery delivery, as I noted in a recent article on the company. Amazon (AMZN), Uber Eats (UBER), and others also add to competitive pressure.

Representatively of rising competition, Walmart’s and Albertsons’ (ACI) ecommerce growth reached 22% in the latest reported quarter, Costco’s (COST) ecommerce growth was 15%, and Kroger’s ecommerce growth was 11%. All experienced growth above Instacart’s Q1 growth in GTV as Instacart is losing market share to competition.

While Instacart has a lead on many online platforms from a first-mover advantage, and benefits from much larger scale than most competitors in grocery delivery, the company’s significant market position clearly isn’t fully protected.

Instacart has recently spent an increasing amount on sales and marketing for customer acquisition to try to combat the market share decline. Sales and marketing expenses reached $216 million in Q1, up by 17% year-on-year. As a percentage of GTV, sales and marketing expenses were up by 20 basis points to 2.4% in the quarter. So far, increased marketing spend doesn’t seem to have snapped the decline in market share as is suggested by the Q2 GTV growth guidance. Despite increased marketing, Instacart’s adjusted EBITDA still grew by 23% in Q1 to $244 million through lower R&D and with operating leverage in other expense items.

Ultimately, Instacart will likely remain one of the largest companies in the space and should continue to achieve good earnings growth through market growth. Recent quarters have continued to show healthy user growth despite rising competitive pressure.

Instacart’s Valuation Already Reflects Market Share Pressure

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model to estimate a fair value for Instacart’s stock.

I expect that Instacart will continue to grow revenues, but at a lower rate than the market as a whole. From 2024 to 2034, I estimate a revenue CAGR of 5.8%. I expect 2.5% perpetual growth after the estimate period.

I believe that Instacart should continue to expand margins through operating leverage, more than offsetting increases in sales and marketing expenses. I estimate the company to ultimately reach a 30% adjusted EBITDA margin. In the cash flow conversion, I assume two thirds of EBITDA to be taxable at a 25% tax rate, and that Instacart spends 2% of annual revenues on capital expenditures and net working capital increases.

Even past massive IPO-related stock-based compensation, or SBC, Instacart’s SBC has remained significant with $357 million worth of SBC during the past twelve months. I have factored SBC into the DCF model by adding 20% dilution to Instacart’s outstanding shares.

The estimates put Instacart’s fair value at $54.45, nearly 20% above the stock price at the time of writing – I believe that Instacart’s market share pressure is already priced in. Even with quite moderate growth in the upcoming decade, well below market growth, the stock has some upside. I don’t believe that the estimated upside is quite enough for a Buy rating, though.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.37%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t estimate Instacart to draw any debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond (US10Y) yield of 4.41% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. Seeking Alpha estimates Instacart’s beta at 1.10. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 9.37%.

Takeaway

Instacart has grown its earnings well as the grocery delivery market has grown. Yet, the company’s growth is below the market's as a whole – many grocery chains have developed their own delivery services, lowering the need for third-party services such as Instacart. Competitive pressure from DoorDash, Uber Eats, and others adds to market share pressure. I still believe that Instacart should achieve good earnings growth in the future, and that a good amount of market share weakness is already priced in. As such, I initiate CART at a Hold rating.

data.title: Marvell: June Event Becomes The Moment Of Truth
data.description:
I started covering Marvell Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MRVL) on Seeking Alpha after their Q4 earnings. At that point, the stock had already taken a significant hit, and I viewed the selloff as an entry opportunity.

It never ceases to amaze me how the stock market reflects a kind of collective understanding—or bet—on a company's future trajectory. Take Tesla, for instance: fundamentals have been deteriorating quarter after quarter, yet the stock continues to climb. Why? Because the market believes robotaxis and humanoid robots will eventually unlock massive revenue streams.

In Marvell’s case, we’ve seen both ends of that narrative spectrum. At one point, investor enthusiasm around AI and custom silicon led to a valuation that arguably priced in too much too soon. Now we’re facing the opposite extreme: a widespread lack of confidence that the company’s model will do fine in the future. And that’s despite the fact that recent quarters have shown clear improvements in earnings.

To me, this dynamic is just another example of why the theory of efficient markets often feels more like a theoretical construct than a reflection of reality. Just look at the price action.

That said, I believe several events have been central to this negative narrative shift:

  1. The departure of Raghib Hussain. Losing the President of Products and Technologies—likely the second most important person at Marvell—to Altera is puzzling. He probably held a significant amount of equity from the Inphi acquisition. Altera is not the fastest-growing company in the world, and its markets are kind of limited too. Why would he make that move if Marvell's roadmap is intact? So yes, it’s a yellow flag. A month ago, two senior board members also quit their seats. While turnover happens, the timing adds to investor unease.

  2. Selling a division to Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY). While this sale is not necessarily a bad thing—especially since it fits the company’s strategy of focusing on data centers and AI—it hadn’t been previously discussed, which raises some speculation around a possible need to reduce debt faster. I don’t hate the move, to be honest. It’s safer to ride with lower debt.

  3. The Amazon (AMZN) roadmap discussion. I guess this is the most important one. The whole thesis for MRVL going forward is its ability to secure next-generation custom silicon deals with big tech. One would expect that after landing one generation, winning the next would be easier—but that’s not always the case. There’s been a lot of talk about Amazon losing Trainium 3 to AIChip, based on supply chain surveys, and in a sense, I believe the company hasn’t been able to dispute that info in a clear way.

  4. SerDes issues and Broadcom (AVGO) competition. There’s also speculation regarding some technical aspects of Marvell’s optics and networking products, with some arguing that Broadcom has closed the gap in performance and differentiation and another change in the broader network industry.

  5. Postponing the Investor Day. Marvell has typically used these events to announce new design wins. Postponing it—right in the middle of all this speculation—isn’t a great look. While I understand that tariffs and macro uncertainty add complexity to future assumptions, delaying a platform meant to showcase progress also adds to the chorus of “they’ve lost Trainium 3 for sure.”

So yes, as the narrative turned, this earnings call was much more about addressing—and possibly shifting—some of these perceptions rather than just delivering an EPS surprise or next-quarter revenue guidance.

Let's dig into the call.

FQ1 2026 Earnings Call

Starting with the numbers, execution remains strong. Revenue came in at $1.90 billion, up 63% year over year, beating guidance by $16 million. EPS grew 158% to $0.62, above consensus by a cent. Operating leverage is clearly in play, with earnings growth outpacing revenue. The data center has lower gross margins but also lower operating costs. Data center continues to be the growth engine, now representing 76% of total revenue, up from 75% last quarter.

Outside of the data center, the picture is mixed but improving. Enterprise networking and carrier infrastructure grew 14% sequentially and are expected to keep recovering—encouraging signs after several sluggish quarters. The consumer was weak, as expected, but management is guiding for a 50% sequential rebound driven by seasonality and gaming demand. Automotive and industrial were essentially flat and likely won’t be strategic going forward, especially after the Infineon divestiture. Overall, this lines up with my thesis that these segments are gradually returning to more historical run-rate levels.

The guide for Q2 was strong: $2 billion at the midpoint, up 57% YoY. Gross margins are expected to remain stable, and capex discipline is evident. But again, none of this was ever the problem.

In the very first question of the call, analyst Vivek Arya asked the question everyone was looking for:

Matt, for clarifying that you have that 3-nanometer program at your large XPU customer and have the follow-on program at the other large customer, just given all the Asia supply chain noise. I guess I had two related questions. One is what is the direction of content in these next-generation programs? Is it up, down, flat? And number two, are you exclusive on these 3-nanometer XPUs or do you expect to share that program given that your competition also seems to be convinced that they have the program. So just comments on content and exclusivity?

Murphy answered that chain chatter doesn’t reflect the full picture. Marvell is the incumbent on the current-gen XPU, already in high-volume production, and they’ve secured 3nm and packaging capacity for the next-gen, which should ramp in 2026. He reaffirmed that revenue from this customer is expected to grow next year and beyond. That said, he also acknowledged that customers may be pursuing multiple paths.

So yes, I think this is as close as it gets to an admission that AIChip probably won at least part of Trainium 3. I also don’t really buy the idea of dual-sourcing an edge chip indefinitely, since it would drive up the cost per unit for both suppliers. But maybe Amazon is prioritizing flexibility and supply chain control over cost efficiency.

In a later question, he also said that Marvell has the “capacity engineering-wise to expand our portfolio and expand our engagements,” which again doesn’t sound like they’ve already secured new designs with other customers.

When questioned about the SerDes Matt gave this time a more confident answer to my view:

On the first topic, we'll actually be showcasing this at our AI Event. Our SerDes technology remains best-in-class, both from a performance standpoint, as well as a time-to-market standpoint (...) It's performing extremely well. And we just showed off at OFC, the first and only 400-gig per lane demonstration with a very aggressive road map there. So that is in great shape.

Near the end of the call we also had another question on the custom chips, and this time I guess the answer was better framed:

I did say that given the volumes, it's certainly possible that there are multiple paths pursued. For us and our business and what I said in the prepared remarks as well we feel very confident and very good about our revenue continuity on the initial program and the revenue ramp on our additional program.” It still doesn’t fully deny the share loss, but it at least gives a bit more clarity on expected revenue.

My Take

The numbers were fine. Better than fine, actually. I do think the recovery in the other segments can help fundamentals and it is still somewhat underrated for now. I was also pleased to see that China's exposure and the tariff landscape weren’t even mentioned on the call, which suggests there’s no material downside from that chaos.

However, the market doesn’t just trade on what happened—it trades on what it thinks will happen, especially when it comes to the data center portion of revenue. And if this call was meant to shift that perception, I’m not sure it fully landed.

At this point, it seems pretty clear that Trainium 3 will be double-sourced, and it’s still uncertain how revenue will be split between the two suppliers. It’s also unclear when the ramp will actually happen, but what is clear is that it will replace Trainium 2—where Marvell had 100%—so the Amazon share will go down.

Again, I guess this kind of implicit admission of share loss just adds to the narrative that the future might look worse than the present.

The event in June could help change that narrative—but now it carries even more weight. Investors will be looking for tangible signs of new design wins, roadmap clarity, and proof that Marvell is still a core partner for next-gen AI infrastructure. Without that, the cloud of uncertainty probably sticks around.

Fiscal Period Ending EPS Estimate YoY Growth 1M Trend 3M Trend 6M Trend

Jan 2026

 2.78 77.27%

-0.51%

-0.13%

9.78%

Jan 2027

 3.57 28.30%

-1.13%

-2.77%

4.18%

Jan 2028

 4.31 20.66%

-4.93%

-7.98%

-14.17%


Sell-side estimates have been adjusting, but not in any brutal way. The real issue was on the buy side—expectations for data center revenue were overly optimistic, which pushed the stock up, only to drag it back down once those projections didn’t materialize.

This quarter’s EPS came in at $0.62, so there aren’t any aggressive growth assumptions baked into the 2026 numbers. Margins should continue to improve as Trainium 2 ramps and matures. Over the past few months, we’ve seen multiple expansions across most AI-related names—except Marvell. That divergence says a lot about the market’s current lack of confidence in the company’s growth story.

All in all, while I don’t like the scenario outlined in the introduction, I also think Marvell’s current relative valuation offers limited downside. Sentiment feels so negative at this point that surprises are more likely to come to the upside.

The key question now is whether Marvell’s share of Trainium 3 will end up delivering more earnings than the 100% share it had in Trainium 2. For now, that’s what Matt is guiding me toward, and I’m giving him the benefit of the doubt.

I’m maintaining my Buy rating going into the June event, even if it feels a bit like a binary outcome I’m not entirely comfortable betting on. At least the current multiples—and the ongoing recovery in mature segments—offer some cushion if the upside case takes longer to play out.

data.title: Tonix Pharmaceuticals: August PDUFA Date For Fibromyalgia Med A Risky Bet
data.description:
Investment Overview

I last covered Tonix Pharmaceuticals Holding Corp. (NASDAQ:TNXP) for Seeking Alpha back in March 2025, issuing a "Hold" recommendation, with stock trading at $30 per share. Shares have, in fact, gained >25% since that call.

The call was based on several factors. I noted that Tonix markets and sells two commercially available drugs to treat acute migraine, Zembrace and Tosymra, but these two drugs make negligible sales - for example, in Q1 2025, their combined revenues totaled $2.4m, down from $2.5m in the prior year.

I also noted the company's overwhelming priority was to secure approval for its lead pipeline candidate, TNX-102 SL, or Cyclobenzaprine HCl Sublingual Tablets, to treat patients with fibromyalgia.

According to the Mayo Clinic:

Fibromyalgia is a long-term condition that involves widespread body pain. The pain happens along with fatigue. It also can involve issues with sleep, memory and mood. Researchers think that fibromyalgia affects the way the brain and spinal cord process painful and nonpainful signals. That increases your overall sensitivity to pain.

According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention ("CDC"), fibromyalgia affects ~4m US adults.

Tonix has submitted its New Drug Application ("NDA") to the FDA based on - according to its Q1 2025 10Q submission - "two statistically significant Phase 3 studies for the management of fibromyalgia," and has been assigned a Prescription Drug User Fee Act (“PDUFA”) goal date of August 15, 2025 i.e. that is the date by which the FDA must rule whether to approve or reject the drug.

When I covered Tonix in March, shares had leaped >200% in value as management shared news that the FDA would not convene an advisory committee ("AdCom"), to discuss and potentially vote on whether TNX-102 SL should be approved for commercial use.

While an AdCom's decision is not binding, the FDA usually - but not always - follows its advice, but when the agency opts against convening an AdCom, it is often interpreted by the market as a sign it has made up its mind to approve the drug.

Finally, however, I noted that:

Tonix completed a 1-for-32 reverse stock split in May 2022, then a 1-for-6.25 reverse stock split in May 2023, a 1-32 split in June last year, and remarkably, a 1-100 split in February this year. Regarding the last split, Tonix stated that it was:

That means that, on a 1-year basis, Tonix stock is down >90%, and its market cap valuation is presently $285m, making it a higher risk investment opportunity.

Tonix has been working on TNX-102 for more than a decade, and prior versions of the drug failed to meet endpoints in key clinical studies (discussed in more detail in my previous post), which explains why the share price has suffered, and why there may be skepticism around whether Tonix can secure approval for TNX-102, and even if it did, whether it could turn its drug into a commercial success.

In April, Tonix stock fell as low as $15 per share, but just a few days ago, had climbed >$42 per share, before dropping ~8% in trading so far today. In this update, I'll update on any developments since my last coverage, try to explain recent share price volatility, and speculate about where I see the share price heading next.

Tonix - Re-Visiting TNX-102 Approval Shot

No new fibromyalgia drugs have been approved in over 15 years - currently approved meds include Eli Lilly's (LLY) Cymbalta, and its generics, Viatris' (VTRS) Lyrica, and its generics, Savella (milnacipran), marketed by AbbVie (ABBV), and cyclobezaprine, the underlying ingredient in TNX-102, sold as Flexeril since 1977 for treatment of muscle spasms, and its generics.

As we can see above (slide taken from a recent Tonix presentation) Tonix has done its market research, which suggests physicians are broadly dissatisfied with current treatment options.

Tonix's solution - a sublingual formulation of cyclobenzaprine ("CBP") seems to make sense. The underlying ingredient is a non-opioid, the sublingual formulation administration induces rapid drug exposure, and a durable (14 week) reduction in pain has been shown in two separate pivotal studies.

While Tonix initially failed with a 2.8mg version, its latest version uses a 5.6mg dose. The FDA gave the drug fast Track Designation in July 2024, and in its investor presentation Tonix provides a lot of detailed information around CBP and its positive treatment effects.

The recent uptrend in the value of shares may suggest the market believes the FDA will approve the drug, however we may note the small share float of 7.32m, plus the fact that ~12% of the share float is apparently held short.

As of Q1, Tonix reported $131.7m of cash, although the company states in its earnings press release that this "will meet its planned operating and capital expenditure requirements into the second quarter of 2026," so investors should probably expect more dilutive fundraising activities. Doubtless, Tonix will hope to raise at a much higher share price in August on the back of an FDA approval. Net loss in Q1 was $(16.83m), or $2.84 per share.

Frankly, I am 50/50 on whether TNX-102 will be approved, but interestingly, Aquestive Therapeutics (AQST) is set to find out any day now whether its sublingual drug candidate Anaphylm™ (epinephrine) will gain approval to treat severe allergic reactions, which could be a useful yardstick to assess the FDA's stance on sublingual medications.

I am personally not completely convinced they are a significant enough improvement on what is already available to warrant approval, and as mentioned, even if TNX-102 is approved, there is limited funding to support a launch campaign, and we may also note multiple drugs are prescribed off-label for fibromyalgia, so persuading patients to switch away from their favoured treatment may be problematic.

Tonix - Opportunities Beyond Lead Candidate

As we can see above, Tonix has initiated a Phase 2 study of TNX-102 SL in acute stress disorder - the dosing of a first patient in this study was announced earlier this month, and may have played a significant role in the recent share price gains.

There is also an anti-CD40 monoclonal antibody in development for organ transplant rejection / autoimmune conditions, and a horsepox vaccine. Make no mistake, TNX-102 is the key value driver here, but at times when outbreaks of Mpox are reported - which has happened on a couple of occasions over the past couple of years - share prices of companies developing antidote's do tend to spike.

While emphasizing the primary focus on TNX-102 in the Q1 earnings release - Tonix CEO Dr Seth Lederman stated that "we are building out our commercial team for the anticipated product launch in the fourth quarter of this year" - the CEO did also state that he was "encouraged" by positive Phase 1 results for TNX-1500, and hoped to share data for this asset and the mpox / smallpox vaccine later this year.

Concluding Thoughts - Volatility Looks Guaranteed - A Speculative Buy?

As mentioned above, while recent share price gains imply positive market sentiment, small purchases of stock can likely create significant upside or downside movements, so it is best not to pay much attention to recent swings.

The August PDUFA data for TNX-102 can only be described as a critical moment for Tonix - succeed, and I suspect shares will spike on news of a first new fibromylagia treatment in 15 years. Fail, and after four reverse stock splits in two years, I suspect Tonix shares would plunge in value, putting the company's future as a public entity in doubt.

Ultimately, since I cannot much in the way of persuasive evidence to suggest TNX-102 will not be approved by the FDA in August, I favor the bull case for share, although only in the short term. I would be less convinced that Tonix can convert its innovative fibromyalgia solution into a profitable, triple-digit million selling product.

I could be wrong of course, and as mentioned previously, it's important to understand that this is a <$300m market cap company with limited finances and therefore represents a very high risk investment opportunity.

I would certainly wait to see what happens with Aquestive before making any moves, but if that company's sublingual product is approved, I'd make Tonix a marginally more "double your money" than "lose your shirt" opportunity, although only by around 55:45, and I'd be looking to sell shares quickly post-approval.

data.title: Major May Rally, Stocks Soar Amid AI Optimism And Tariff Relief
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) ended just shy of posting its best May since 1990. Stocks tallied strong gains, despite ongoing uncertainties surrounding the trade war. Indeed, traders appear to be largely looking past changing tariff headlines, instead focusing on what turned out to be a stellar Q1 earnings season and consumers who continue to spend. Oh, an AI is back in vogue, too.

The S&P 500 ETF (SPY) tacked on 6%, while the Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ) rose a strong 9%. U.S. small caps underperformed modestly, but the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) returned 5%.

International stocks built on the big 2025 advance. The Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets ETF (VEA) jumped more than 5%, while the Vanguard Emerging Markets ETF (VWO) added 4%.

In the bond market, the iShares Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG) was down 1% amid Treasury rates that backed up through much of May. Yields did stabilize over the back half of the month, however. On a year-over-year basis, AGG has returned a respectable 5.5%, including dividends.

Elsewhere, bitcoin (BTC-USD, IBIT) was up 10% in May but finished the month well off its new all-time high. Gold (GLD) was flat, along with sideways action in the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY). An early-month bump helped oil score a 4% rally.

Stocks Charged Higher in May, U.S. Large Caps Led

Digging into the sectors, it was all about tech and AI. The best of the 11 S&P 500 areas was Information Technology (XLK). The largest sector rose 9%. Runner-up was Industrials (XLI), driven higher by enduring strength from the Aerospace & Defense industry and cyclical stocks exposed to AI—perhaps we should add GE Aerospace (GE) and GE Vernova (GEV) to the list of so-called Magnificent stocks.

Bringing up the rear—by a wide margin—was Health Care (XLV). UnitedHealth Group (UNH) plunged amid regulatory concerns, while Eli Lilly (LLY) drifted toward the bottom of its now year-long trading range.

Broadly, 20%-plus rallies from Tesla (TSLA) and Nvidia (NVDA) helped U.S. large caps to a strong May showing. Only Apple (AAPL) was down among the Mag 7.

Tech Led in May, Health Care Was the Lone Negative Sector

Driving price action has been resilient macro data. The April jobs report was yet another in a string of generally "good, not great" views of the labor market. Though the unemployment rate ticked up marginally, the headline establishment survey employment gain was close to 200,000 for a second consecutive month.

Average hourly earnings ticked down to 3.8%, helping to assuage inflation concerns. Ahead of the May NFP report, due out next Friday, expectations call for a payroll gain of about 140,000 with an unchanged jobless rate.

U.S. Labor Remains Good, Not Great

The consumer remains in focus, given the precarious situation around tariffs. As Liberation Day came and went, it was clear that households and businesses were stockpiling to avoid potentially much higher price tags on goods that were supposed to arrive in May. So far, high-frequency consumption data point to ongoing spending strength. We’ll know more when the May Retail Sales report is released in mid-June.

For now, Johnson Redbook Retail Sales data, OpenTable reservation bookings, and record-high TSA checkpoint numbers point to solid spending activity.

U.S. Retail Sales Strong at +5.2% YoY

On the inflation front, there was good news on May’s final trading day. PCE Price Index figures for April revealed a continued trend of disinflation. The year-on-year headline PCE Price Index change ticked down to 2.1%, the lowest in four years, and within a whisker of the Fed’s 2% target.

Of course, expectations are for a bump up in both PCE inflation and the CPI rate as we progress through the summer and the second half of 2025. Overall, the market expects a 3.4% year-on-year headline CPI by the end of the year.

PCE Price Index Inflation YoY Dips to 2.1%

The most encouraging data point during May? Implied recession odds. According to online prediction markets, the chance of a technical U.S. recession (two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth) plunged from 70% on April 30 to 33% by May 30.

A much better-than-expected goods trade deficit report on Friday helped support what’s likely to be a 2%+ GDP print for the current quarter.

U.S. Recession Probability Halved

Put it all together, and the Fed is likely to do nothing for the foreseeable future. The effective Fed Funds rate is seen holding at 4.33% at least through July, with just a modest chance of a quarter-point September ease. It will make Chair Powell’s Jackson Hole speech all the more interesting.

That presentation will come after what’s likely to be a series of nudges from President Trump calling on the Fed chief to cut rates sooner rather than later. Powell met with Trump at the White House last Thursday.

Fed Seen Holding Steady Through August

Despite a better macroeconomic backdrop compared to two months ago, strategists keep slashing their current-year S&P 500 EPS forecasts.

Now below $265 (with out-year per-share earnings estimates under $300), the SPX’s price-to-earnings ratio is above 21, not far from its early-year highs.

S&P 500 EPS Trend Remains Lower

Finally, investors should be aware that we are in the throes of bullish seasonality. Readers of my analyses know that I like to shed light on calendar trends, and for the S&P 500 ETF, the period from May to July has been particularly strong.

The ETF, excluding dividends, has been up in 26 of the past 28 months, from May through July. June tends to be a less strong month, averaging a 0.8% price advance.

S&P 500 Leans Bullish Through July

The Bottom Line

The stock market has been resilient since hitting its April 7 post-Liberation Day intrasession low. May was a banner month, with global stocks notching record highs despite the interest rate rise. Economic data has been strong, though the so-called “soft” survey data remains bleak.

Going forward, all eyes will be on President Trump’s Truth Social page and each passing macro data point.

data.title: Solana Price Analysis: Can Bulls Break Above Recent Highs Of $185?
data.description:
By Christian Norman

Year-to-date, 2025 has proven to be an interesting time for the crypto market, with Solana being no exception.

Enjoying a period of bullish momentum bookmarked by Trump’s win in the election, seen as the pro-crypto candidate of choice, Solana not only rose to an all-time high just shy of $300 but overtook Binance Coin (BNB) to become the fifth-largest coin by market capitalization globally.

Currently trading at 179.38, a ~39% discount from all-time highs made in mid-January, the recent fall in Solana pricing has sparked interest amongst those looking for a buying opportunity.

Notwithstanding, this decline in value is not exclusive to Solana, with much of the crypto space feeling the full force of waning market risk appetite.

With a meteoric rise in crypto value still fresh in the collective memory, the $1,000,000 question becomes whether this phenomenon will continue in 2025, or whether general market risk aversion will bode negatively for crypto— Solana included.

What’s next for Solana?

SOLUSD: Technical analysis

  • Moving averages: Currently, long-term moving averages such as the 100 and 200-period suggest bullish directional bias, with recent price action breaking above key levels of support. This suggests that Solana has found sustained buyer pressure in the medium to long-term, and should remain in a general uptrend should current momentum continue.
  • Oscillators lean neutral to sell: Many of the most commonly used trading indicators currently lean towards either a neutral or bearish bias. The Relative Strength Index (RSI) and Stochastics currently or recently have Solana as ‘overbought.’ At the same time, the MACD shows a weakening bull trend, with the distance between the MACD and signal line narrowing. Increased US rate cut bets are also benefiting silver pricing, with markets increasingly anticipating two interest rate cuts in 2025
  • Key levels: At the time of writing, Solana trades rangebound between ~$165 and ~$184. Historically, this is a range where bull trends are either made or broken. A sustained break above this level would suggest further bullish momentum, while the opposite could impose further downside from January’s highs.

SOLUSD: Fundamental analysis

  • Surge in DEX trading volume: Recent data has shown a significant increase in Solana’s weekly decentralized trading volume (DEX), outperforming many other large-cap crypto coins. This growth in volume suggests higher levels of user activity, liquidity, and growing utility for the Solana network, which is encouraging for Solana in the medium to long term.
  • Crypto strategic reserve: A pledge by Donald Trump as part of his 2024 campaign, Solana is included in the US government’s “Digital Asset Stockpile”. Once a crypto-sceptic, Trump has not only overseen the creation of a United States crypto reserve but also released his project on the Solana blockchain - the now-infamous $TRUMP coin. Although some, including Solana’s co-founder, have reservations about governmental endorsement of crypto, the short-term effects on Solana pricing have historically been positive.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Helmerich & Payne: Best-In-Class Driller Trading At Trough Multiples
data.description:
Summary

Helmerich & Payne (NYSE:HP) is one of the world’s largest onshore drilling contractors. It owns and operates industry leading rigs. The company has been penalized heavily for both macro and company-related reasons such as reducing global active rigs, one-off integration cost and Saudi rig suspensions. However, there are still many reasons to be bullish about the company such as its strong backlog, future synergies savings, best-in-class high margin rigs and depressed multiples. Therefore, buying a small position at this level when the market is fearful while getting a decent dividend yield is not a bad idea.

Business Model

Essentially, HP owns the best-in-class drilling rigs. As Exploration & Production firms like Shell and BP need to drill oil and gas wells, they would rent its rigs for a daily fee which is called Day Rates as they do not want these assets on their balance sheet. Moreover, HP also provides performance-based contracts for their rigs. This means that they can earn an extra ~$1-2k/day (find in page 10) for renting out their drillers if they can drill wells more efficiently. Thus, HP would be incentivized to upgrade their tools in analytics capabilities and technology to reduce cost while increasing profitability. H&P also sells software tools and after market services through a recurring subscription plan to offset some cyclicality in their drilling revenue. Lastly, they also have an asset-light offshore drilling business, where they provide their services for a fixed fee.

HP is the largest driller in the US, leading in both margins and market share. In fact, its margins are becoming increasingly higher than its peers over the years, and they are ~40% higher (find in page 8 of Q2 2025 earnings report). This is mainly driven by its FlexRigs which are built for performance where they can handle faster drilling and lower maintenance cost. Moreover, 50% of its North America contracts are performance-based, allowing HP to earn more for its efficiency, compared to its peers which have no upside for efficiency gain.

As these rigs generate cash, HP is able to return cash to shareholders through dividends while paying down its acquisition debt. Therefore, HP buys and operates efficient drilling rigs, ensuring good performance. Then the cash received is used to allocate to better technology and rewarding shareholders.

Reviewing Q2 2025

HP delivered a slight beat in terms of revenue by $60.6mil and a miss in EPS due to one-off transaction charges and tax lumpiness. However, its segment results are very healthy. The North America solutions segment exceeded the guidance range and contributed directly to the margin of $266mil. Secondly, its International solutions segment contributed a direct margin of $27mil from a $7mil loss in Q1 despite suspensions, well above its guidance. Lastly, its offshore rigs produced $26mil of direct margin, well within the upper range of guidance.

Therefore, all three segments had positive results and that led to ~$222 EBITDA and positive cash flow from operations. In fact, management is bullish on long-term fundamentals especially with their impressive backlog of $7.6bn, superior margins for further growth.

Valuation and Investment Thesis

HP is currently trading at a deeply depressed level relative to both historical norms and the sector median of its peers. What is even more striking is its TTM EV/EBITDA and Price/Sales are trading at an 81% and 73% discount from its 5-year average respectively.

I think this huge valuation gap reflects market concern over the entire sector, especially with falling overall rig counts. According to Baker Huges, the North American land rigs fell to 566 rigs from 600 rigs since last year. This means there is lower demand which would flow to lesser revenue overall. An under-utilized fleet would also mean margin compression as the fixed cost fleet is spread over lesser operating rigs. Since last year, its peer’s day-rates have declined ~7% (find in page 8 of Q2 2025 earnings report). Of course, this has also led to a decline in free cash flow. Therefore, these factors have led to very negative sentiment, and its share price has reduced back to 2020 Covid lows.

However, 50% of HP North American fleet is under performance contracts which have higher-margins, and they could also mothball lower-spec fleets while keeping the higher margin fleet active. In Q2, HP’s cashflow reduced to $56M due to one-off acquisition costs, which compressed free cash flow. However, these charges are non-recurring and HP expects $50-70M annual cost savings synergies which would lead to lower rig cost moving forward.

Also, HP has many enduring strengths that the market is not appreciating as much, such as its massive backlog of $7.6bn which provides multi-year revenue visibility. Moreover, its dividend yield of 6.9% is also higher than the sector median with its payout ratio below 50% for FY24.

With success in the North America performance-based contracts, HP could also broker some of its contracted rigs in Mexico and Brazil to be under the performance-based contracts as well. This would yield incremental EBITDA, without additional capital expenditure. This could happen because management is finding new avenues to grow internationally, and it would make logical sense to copy what worked and use the same model elsewhere.

Risk

In every energy company, the risk is tied to falling oil prices. As oil prices serve as a function of demand and when oil prices fall too low, there would be less incentive for E&P companies to borrow drilling rigs from HP and that may lead to day rates compression and even lesser contract revenue.

Management also has no clear view when the Saudi Rigs would be restarted and for good reasons. Depressed oil prices have reduced drilling economics and prompting Aramco to defer rig deployment until a sustained price recovery to trigger capital spend commitments.

Conclusion

Despite near term headwinds in US rig count drops, integration costs and Saudi rig suspensions, I think the market has already priced in this information. Also, HP’s top tier margins, strong backlog and decent dividend yield remain intact. With possible expanded performance-contracts and depressed multiples, I think there is good upside when the cycle turns. Thus, I think buying a small position now is not a bad idea.
